aliases: blended iced coffee with whipped cream

english: blended iced coffee with whipped cream

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class (FOODON_03301521); no specific frappuccino or blended iced coffee subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers coffee-based beverages broadly; no specific frappuccino or blended iced coffee code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for blended iced coffee frappuccino. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of a coffee frappuccino is one medium cup of approximately 350 ml, equivalent to roughly 350 g, as commonly served at specialty coffee outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee Frappuccino — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coffee Frappuccino (cr_id D08020004), which reports per-100g values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 14209 (Coffee, prepared from grounds, with milk and sugar) as the closest approved analogue, documented below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a coffee frappuccino in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on the closest approved analogous food: sweetened milk-based coffee beverage (GI ~53, per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 6.68 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) = (53 × 23.38 g) / 100 ≈ 12.4 g GL per 350 g serving. GI 53 is classified as low GI (55 or less).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.21 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite low sodium (28 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit coffee frappuccinos due to the added sugar load (6.21 g/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold); request a sugar-free syrup version or substitute with unsweetened black coffee to eliminate the BP-elevating sugar burden (AHA 2018 guidelines).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 6.21 g/100 g in a liquid beverage with only 0.06 g/100 g dietary fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid regular coffee frappuccinos; the high sugar content (6.21 g/100 g) with negligible fibre (0.06 g/100 g) causes rapid glucose absorption, and the estimated GI of 53 with a glycemic load of ~12.4 per serving represents a significant glycaemic challenge — request a sugar-free, no-syrup version or choose unsweetened coffee alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whipped cream and whole milk (~3.18 g/100 g estimated) combined with added sugar (6.21 g/100 g) contributes to atherogenic lipid profile and endothelial inflammation risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit coffee frappuccinos due to the combined saturated fat (estimated ~3.18 g/100 g from whipped cream and milk) and added sugar content, both of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; opt for a skimmed-milk, no-whipped-cream, no-syrup version to substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.21 g/100 g) and saturated fat from whipped cream promote pro-inflammatory mediators, negating the anti-inflammatory chlorogenic acid polyphenols naturally present in espresso.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the standard coffee frappuccino formulation as the added sugar and saturated fat from whipped cream promote systemic inflammation; plain black coffee or an Americano provides the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A coffee frappuccino is a blended iced coffee beverage made with espresso, chocolate syrup, whole milk, and ice, finished with a topping of whipped cream. It is a cold, creamy, sweet drink with a rich coffee-chocolate flavour profile and a smooth, thick, milkshake-like texture. The beverage is widely available at specialty coffee chains and cafes across Singapore and internationally. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 80 kcal, 2.27 g protein, 5.11 g fat (of which an estimated 3.18 g is saturated fat from the milk and whipped cream), and 6.68 g carbohydrate of which 6.21 g is sugar. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.06 g/100 g, and sodium is low at 28 mg/100 g. Calcium is notable at 72 mg/100 g, contributed by the milk component. Clinically, the high sugar content (6.21 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The estimated glycemic index of 53 (low) and glycemic load of approximately 12.4 per 350 g serving represent a meaningful postprandial glucose challenge. The saturated fat from whipped cream and whole milk further elevates cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. Healthier modifications include requesting sugar-free syrup, omitting whipped cream, and substituting skimmed milk.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a coffee frappuccino?
Is coffee frappuccino suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of a frappuccino?
Is frappuccino good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08020004, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from the closest USDA FoodData Central approved analogue and documented. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (sweetened milk-based coffee beverage) per Atkinson et al. 2008, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.18 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central NDB 14209 (Coffee, prepared from grounds, with milk and sugar) as the closest approved analogue to a milk-and-cream-based blended coffee beverage; the HPB source does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific blended coffee preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact frappuccino match as of 2026. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based coffee beverage per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008, Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for coffee frappuccino exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 53 is classified as low GI (55 or less). 4. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.5 and source 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from an approved GI source. 5. The input dietary_tags included 'low_calorie' (80 kcal/100 g is borderline) and 'low_sodium' (28 mg/100 g, which is accurate); these are retained as contextual notes only and not used as clinical claims. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a Western/International specialty coffee beverage. Mandatory multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, etc.) are not required under the G6 multilingual naming rule for this cuisine category. All local_names fields are set to null accordingly.
